X1.1.label: Question X1.1
X1.1.scenario: Long-term care insurance policies can provide benefits in the form of the care that the insured needs, or in the form of a guaranteed cash benefit payable when an insured event occurs.
X1.1.synopsis: Long-term care policies: Cash vs care benefits, pre-funded vs immediate needs design, and low new business volume analysis.

(i).question: Outline the advantages and disadvantages for the insurer and the insured of policies with a cash benefit.
(i).synopsis: Focus on cost reduction and risk management for the insurer, and premium affordability vs. care cost inflation for the insured.

For the insurer: Cash benefits will reduce claim administration costs (eg no pre-authorisation of expenditure, no processing of invoices for care), and so makes premiums cheaper. [½]
Only incidence rates of insured events have to be estimated. [½]
No assumption about the claim size distribution is required. [½]
There is no need to estimate the expected rate of inflation in care costs. [½]
There is less risk for the insurer and so contingency loadings can be lower, resulting in lower premiums. [½]
Lower premiums may lead to increased sales volumes. [½]
It may be possible to offer budget policies with modest cash benefits (and modest premiums) that are designed to contribute to care costs rather than aiming to provide the total costs, ... [½]
... which would widen the market for this type of policy. [½]
There may be pressure on the insurer to provide advice on spending the cash benefits in the care market. [½]

If cash benefits are not sufficient to provide adequate care, the insurer may be blamed, particularly if there is any evidence from sales literature or the sales process that the product may have been mis-sold. [½]
For the insured: All other things being equal, premiums will be less than for care benefits because contingency loadings will be smaller. [½]
The insured can choose to spend the money on anything, which provides the flexibility to choose formal care to complement any informal care that is available. [½]
The amount of cash may not be sufficient to cover the full cost of care unless the sums insured are kept up to date. [½]
The insured (possibly with the help of relatives) will be responsible for buying care when the insured is frail and much less able to make effective decisions. [½]
The insured or relatives are unlikely to have experience of buying care, and so may not be aware of all the available alternatives, nor of prices of care etc. [½]

(ii).question: Describe the differences between a pre-funded and an immediate needs long-term care insurance policy.
(ii).synopsis: Contrast health states at purchase (fit vs frail), premium types (regular/single), and benefit payout certainty.

Pre-funded policy: This is a protection insurance policy. [½]
The policy is bought when the insured is in good health. [½]
The benefit can be cash or provide indemnity against the costs of care. [½]
Premiums may be: a single premium paid at the outset [½]
regular premiums, which are paid until the earlier of when benefits are paid, death and policy expiry... [1]
... and may be level or increasing in a pre-determined way. [½]
There is a possibility that the insured will pay premiums and receive no benefits. [½]
The most basic products do not offer a surrender benefit. [½]
In some cases, where regular premiums have been paid for a minimum period, a paid-up benefit may be available, which would normally reduce the benefit amount. [½]
The policy may be unit-linked or conventional. [½]
There may be a death benefit (eg return of premiums). [½]
Immediate needs policy: This can be expressed as a form of impaired life annuity. [½]
The policy is bought when the insured is in poor health, and in need of care. [½]
Benefits are usually in the form of regular cash income, which might be designed to meet the expected costs of care. [½]
A single premium is paid at outset. [½]
The insured will always receive some benefits. [½]
Policies will not acquire a surrender value at any time. [½]
However, some policies may offer a capital guarantee (eg purchase price less benefits received is paid on death). This will increase the purchase price for a given benefit level. [½]

(iii).question: Describe the advantages and disadvantages for the insured of each of these designs (ie pre-funded and immediate needs long-term care).
(iii).synopsis: PF: Early commitment vs asset protection. IN: Immediate liquidity vs interest rate/inflation risk.

Pre-funded LTCI policy: PF LTCI provides protection against the cost of long-term care for as long as this is required in old age. [½]
It reduces the financial burden on the insured's family and/or protects the insured's assets (eg the family home), which might otherwise need to be used or sold to pay for care. [½]
There can be a limited premium payment term (eg premiums cease at age 85), which helps the insured to meet the costs more easily. [½]
Premiums may be relatively modest if the policy is taken out at a young age ... [½]
... however, generally, cover may be expensive, and hence unaffordable, due to the margins required etc. [½]
The payment of regular premiums will be a long-term commitment for the insured, ... [½]
... which may be at a time when there may be other, more important, demands on the insured's finances (eg personal pension, other insurances or mortgage repayments). [½]
Premiums are rarely guaranteed, and so premiums may be reviewed (upwards) every five years, say, which makes it difficult for the insured to budget. [½]
A PF LTCI policy will usually have no surrender value and the insured will lose out if they need to lapse the policy – for example, as a result of changing circumstances such as the loss of job. [½]
Immediate needs policy: An immediate needs policy helps protect against risk that savings will not be sufficient to provide continuing care for remaining lifetime, or that one's estate will be used up in meeting the cost of care. [½]

Specialist products (eg indemnity-based or with different levels of cover) may also protect against increasing cost of care due to further deterioration of health and/or future inflation of care costs. [½]
The policy fulfils an immediate need, and so there is no possibility of premiums being ‘wasted’ because benefits will always be paid. [½]
Benefits are usually in the form of cash, and so are not guaranteed to cover the total cost of care. [½]
The insured will need to find a large sum to pay the single premium ... [½]
... this may require assets to be realised or money to be borrowed against assets (eg the insured's home). [½]
The cost of the policy will depend on the financial circumstances at the time it is bought (eg premiums will be higher if interest rates are low at the time). [½]
The insured has no control over the time that the policy will be needed, and so may turn out to be short of assets when they are required. [½]

(iv).question:
A developed country has historically experienced very low new business volumes for its pre-funded long-term care insurance products.

Suggest reasons why there may be low pre-funded long-term care insurance new business volumes in this particular country.

Issues with the product: The pre-funded long-term care insurance (PF LTCI) products may be relatively expensive. [½]
This may restrict which members of the population that are able to afford the product leading to low new business volumes. [½]
If the price is high it may also be viewed as poor value for money by consumers. [½]
It may be that immediate needs LTCI is preferred within the market and as such there is low demand for PF LTCI. [½]
There may be very few providers of PF LTCI in the country. [½]
This may lead to a lack of public awareness about PF LTCI. [½]
The lack of providers could be due to the level of barriers for entry in the market, for example if there is lots of legislation or regulation around the product, or high capital requirements. [½]
This would lead to a low capacity in the insurance market for PF LTC risks. [½]
Issues with providers: The cause of the low new business volumes could be related to a poor perception of PF LTCI providers. [½]
This could be due to adverse media coverage, perhaps related to issues with declined claims. [½]
It could also be due to issues with mis-selling, resulting in fines for insurers. [½]
Issues with the target market: There may be a lack of demand within the expected target market for PF LTCI. [½]
This could be due to a lack of understanding about the costs of long-term care and the potential benefit of taking out insurance such as this. [½]
It could also be due to an expectation that the State will care for individuals once they require long-term care. [½]
This may be a perception or it could be the actual current position. [½]

X1.2.label: Question X1.2
X1.2.scenario:
A consulting actuary is providing advice to a large employer concerning its health and care employer benefits.

The employer has about 1,200 employees all based in a developed country with a comprehensive free State healthcare service and unemployment benefits. It plans to introduce a flexible benefits package for all staff. Each employee will be allocated annually a fund which may be used to purchase benefits. The fund will be calculated as the notional value of the current benefits given to employees. The fund can be used to purchase additional holiday, gym membership, travel insurance cover, life assurance cover and a range of health insurance benefits (excluding income protection).

Employees can increase the value of their fund by reducing any existing benefits or holiday. Any fund value not spent will be added to basic salary.
X1.2.synopsis: Flex-benefits logic: Focus on employer benefits (PMI, CI, etc.), employee choice, costs of self-insurance, and medical inflation drivers.

(i).question: Outline suitable health insurance benefits which might be included in the overall package and options that could be considered for the overall package. You should include a brief description of the cover.

Suitable health insurance benefits: Critical illness insurance – a lump sum (which could be linked to salary) paid on the diagnosis of one of a specified list of critical illnesses. [1]
This could be accelerated (if no other death in service benefit is offered) or stand-alone. [½]
Private medical insurance (PMI) – pays out on certain medical events, generally an indemnity benefit that pays for all costs of care. [1]
If there is a comprehensive State healthcare system it could take the form of a waiting list plan. [½]
Dental cover – pays a fixed amount for dental treatment. [1]
Optical cover – pays a fixed amount for optical tests, and may also include a contribution towards any glasses that may be required. [1]
Personal accident – fixed benefit amount paid in the event that the individual suffers the loss of one or more limbs, or other specified injury. [1]
Health cash plan – pays a pre-specified cash sum on the occurrence of certain medical events (eg hospital stays). [½]
Typically benefits paid are low relative to the cost of medical expenses. [½]

Major medical expenses – in the UK, this is a variant of PMI that pays a fixed amount (which can vary with severity) to policyholders who undergo surgery of a non-investigative and non-cosmetic nature. [1]
All cover needs to be easy to understand and simple as there is unlikely to be face-to-face selling. [½]
Options for cover: covering the employee only or offering family cover too [½]

(ii).question: Explain why income protection cover is usually provided as part of the basic employment benefits rather than as part of a flexible benefits package.

Why IP is offered as basic benefit: Group IP insurance may have significant benefits to employer. [½]
For example, there may be rehabilitation benefits to help get individuals back to work quicker. [½]
It may also help reflect the paternalistic view of the employer towards its staff. [½]
This could assist with the recruitment of key staff. [½]
It may be a legal requirement for an employer to provide IP insurance. [½]
Where this is the case, group IP insurance will be cheaper than individual cover. [½]
If IP insurance was provided through flexible benefits instead there would be a higher risk of anti-selection for the insurer, leading to higher premiums for those that took out cover. [½]
It would also be difficult to manage as there may be several different occupational classes within the company that require different prices. [½]

(iii).question: Outline possible options and design features if income protection cover had been included in the flexible benefit arrangement.

IP benefit design options and features: If the benefit is included as a flexible benefit, the insurer will need to consider ways to manage the increased risk of anti-selection, compared to the non-flexible benefits product. [1]
Benefit level: Likely to have a lower replacement ratio than non-flexible benefits. [½]
Could increase the benefit level on serious disability (eg failure of ADLs). [½]
Link to a relevant inflation index during claim (eg RPI) to ensure benefit meets the individual's needs. [½]
Could include pension fund contributions and other insurance policies as additional benefit amounts. [½]
Period of payment: Could restrict payment period to a set period eg 5 years, or reduce the benefit level after sickness has occurred for a given period [½]
Ensure that any expiry age is consistent with the normal retirement date used by the company. [½]
Claim definition: Individuals may want an own occupation definition, particularly for any specialist occupations. [½]
Could use own occupation for an initial period and then any suited occupation after a set period. [½]
Deferred period: Ensure that any deferred period is consistent with any statutory benefits offered by the employer or the State. [½]
May want a longer deferred period than for non-flexible benefit products to deter fraudulent claims. [½]
Tiered deferred periods could also be used to provide different levels of cover for different durations of sickness. [½]
Proportionate benefits / rehabilitation: To encourage individuals back to work, ensure that proportional and rehabilitation benefits are offered where appropriate. [½]
This will also help to identify fraudulent claims. [½]
Linked claims: Offering linked claims periods where benefits can re-start if the same illness occurs within a certain period of returning to work may also help encourage earlier returns to work. [½]

(iv).question:
Currently, its group private medical insurance is provided by an insurer based in the same country.

Outline a typical schedule of benefits provided under a group private medical insurance scheme.

Hospital costs: accommodation, nursing care, operating theatre, diagnostic procedures, surgical dressings and drugs for in-patient treatment [½ for each 2 examples]
specialist consultations and physiotherapy received as an in-patient [½]
accommodation, nursing care, operating theatre, diagnostic procedures, surgical dressings and drugs for day care operation not involving an overnight stay [½ for each 2 examples]
accommodation for one parent to stay in hospital with an insured dependant under 12 years old [½]
Specialist Fees: surgeons’ and anaesthetists’ fees for in-patient and day care operations [½]
Outpatient treatment: diagnostic procedures such as radiology and pathology [½]
Other features: recuperative care, to include nursing and domestic services, immediately following in-patient surgical treatment covered by the product and where the stay in hospital was for a minimum period of 7 nights [½]
cash alternatives if the State healthcare service is used [½]
access to private out-of-hours GP and/or telephone helpline [½]
cover restricted to acute illnesses only [½]

(v).question:
The company has recently taken on a director from the USA who is concerned that private medical insurance is being purchased for benefits which might otherwise be provided by the State health service.

Describe the points to be made to this director as to why this arrangement is provided for all employees.

Why benefits are provided for all employ: Although the State health service is free for all citizens, there may be waiting lists for: doctor and hospital appointments, tests, treatments. [½ for 2 examples]
Where an employee has a debilitating but non-life threatening condition this could be a significant period of time. [½]
PMI reduces the overall medical treatment times and thus reduces staff absenteeism, which reduces overall costs and/or increases productivity. [½]
Further, some advanced cancer and other treatments may not be available through the State, or they may not be available in all locations. [½]
Another key attraction of PMI is that if offers more choice to individuals over: hospital / provider, doctor / surgeon, time of appointment. [½ for 2 examples]

There may be a perception that private hospitals offer a better quality of treatment, including: cleaner hospitals (possible reduced risk of infections eg MRSA), better quality of accommodation, higher quality of care. [½ for 2 examples]
PMI can provide cover when employees are travelling overseas. [½]
Offering group PMI may also help the employer to attract and retain good quality staff. [½]
Particularly if other employers within its industry offer PMI cover as part of their remuneration packages. [½]
For employees, group cover will be a cheaper way of accessing PMI than individual cover. [½]
Ultimately group PMI cover is offered because the employee values it higher than the cost to the employer. [½]

(vi).question:
Based on the US experience in group private medical insurance the director from the USA is concerned that the premium rates for this insurance will rise in the future at a rate faster than retail price inflation.

Suggest reasons why this might happen.

Reasons PMI premium may increase faster: Medical cost inflation is a significant driver of the PMI claim costs. [½]
This may increase at a higher rate than RPI due to:
new technology improving the quality of treatment offered for a particular illness [½]
new treatments becoming available where previously there were none available [½]
new diagnostic techniques being developed at a higher cost than previous testing methods [½]
The cost of claims may also increase due to:
higher demand for private medical treatment [½]
higher demand for medical staff, leading to high wage inflation [½]
increasing legal disputes for some claims leading to court award inflation. [½]
There may be an increase in claims volumes leading to higher claim costs. [½]
This could be due to: longer waiting times for State treatment, new tests/treatments/drugs only being available privately, greater awareness of PMI. [½ for 2 examples]
The premium charged to the group will not just be made up of the cost of claims. Loadings for commission, reinsurance and expenses may also increase faster than RPI. [½]
If the scheme is experience rated, poor past experience may lead to a higher increase in premiums. [½]

(vii).question:
The company has concerns regarding the current cost of group private medical insurance and has suggested that this risk is retained internally.

Discuss the key advantages and disadvantages of this approach.

Advantages & Disadvantages of self-insur: Any favourable experience will benefit the company. [½]
The company will not pay out commission to a broker. [½]
The company will not have to pay the insurer's profit margins. [½]
The company will have control over all aspects of the benefits and will not be limited to what is available in the insurance market. [½]
This may reduce costs as the company will only be providing the cover it wants / needs and will not have to pay for additional cover. [½]
The risk remains with the employer hence poor experience will impact directly on the company and its profits. [½]
As this is the only scheme the company will have, claims would be expected to be volatile, leading to more volatile profits. [½]
The company may lack expertise in managing group PMI claims. [½]
It may require specialist skills in claims management and will incur costs recruiting appropriate individuals with these skills. [½]
Alternatively, claims operations can be sub-contracted to a specialist organisation, reducing the company's profits and control through managing the scheme internally. [½]
The company may lack benefit design expertise. [½]
The company may not be able to provide other services provided by insurers eg employee assistance services. [½]

X1.3.label: Question X1.3
X1.3.scenario:
A health and care insurer currently sells income protection insurance through insurance intermediaries. It pays all intermediaries the same commission.

The company is considering using alternative distribution channels to sell its IP insurance.
X1.3.synopsis: Distribution & Commission: Compare channels (direct vs broker), fee-based vs commission-based impacts, claims management interactions, and claim case reviews.

(i).question: Outline the considerations for the company when deciding on whether to use alternative distribution channels.

Cost: The insurer will need to consider the cost of changing the distribution channel used or using additional channels. [½]
For example, it will have established processes and systems for selling its IP product via intermediaries and would need to set up similar processes and systems for any new channels used. [½]
If it is considering moving to setting up its own direct salesforce, this would lead to high costs associated with setting up the channel. [½]

These costs may include: recruiting and training staff for the own direct salesforce or for telesales, setting up a website for internet sales, advertising for brand awareness (as the company no longer has the IFA's recommendation). [half for any sensible example, maximum 1]
Selling through intermediaries will lead to the insurer paying commission for sales made. [½]
Moving to a direct marketing such as online sales would lead to costs such as setting up a website and advertising the channel. [½]
If it does sell via a different channel (eg online), costs per policy sold may be lower, leading to reduced costs and either a reduction in premiums charged to customers or higher profits. [½]
Customer need: The insurer should consider what channels customer want to purchase IP insurance through. [½]
For example, customers many want advice before purchasing IP insurance, and so would not want to purchase it online. [½]
The insurer should also consider whether its product design is suitable to be sold via all channels. For example, if it is very complex, it may not think online sales are appropriate. [½]
Target market: The insurer should consider the potential target market that would purchase insurance through alternative channels. [½]
The likely target market for different distribution channels may vary by: age, gender, occupation, financial sophistication, location. [½ for each 2 examples, max 1 mark]
This could lead to an impact on the insurer's future experience, including on its claim and lapse experience. [½]
If the insurer sells to customers that do not understand its product, it could lead to an increase in complaints and potentially reputational risk. [½]
Competitors: The insurer should consider what channels other IP insurance products in the market use. [½]
For example, if these are only sold via intermediaries, there may not be a market for using alternative channels. [½]
This could lead to a reduction in the insurer's new business volumes. [½]
However, if other providers use alternative channels, it may need to consider alternative channels too in order to remain competitive. [½]
Other: The insurer should consider the impact that using alternative channels would have on its existing intermediaries. [½]
It should also consider the availability of suitable institutions such as banks that would be willing to act as a tied agent. [½]

(ii).question: Suggest possible reasons why the insurer may be considering paying different rates of commission to different intermediaries.

Experience: Insurers may pay different commission rates where they see a difference in the quality of the business being sold by various advisers. [½]
This may be in the form of the: claim inception experience [½], claim recovery rates [½], lapse experience [½], underwriting administration experience. [½]
Where the claim inception experience is seen to differ between adviser this could be passed on via the premium or the commission. [½]
Remuneration: It may be that some intermediaries get other sources of funds from the insurer, eg they may get a fixed sum per month on top of the commission paid. [½]
Some advisers may already charge partial fees so that they don't require full remuneration via commission. [½]
The insurer may be concerned about its ability to clawback any commission if necessary. It may reduce commission as a result. [½]
Target market: The insurer may have a strategic aim to increase market share in some areas and so might have increased commission in those areas. [½]
For example, the insurer may wish to increase the number of large policies it writes or it may wish to reduce risk concentration and focus on smaller policies. [½]
Or it may want to reduce its exposure to a specific industry and so offer lower commission rates to advisers in those geographic locations. [½]
Different advisers will target different population segments and so the insurer can try and use this to achieve the desired mix of business. [½]
The adviser may have a different mix of business by product features or term. [½]
For example, one adviser may sell mainly shorter term IP insurance (5-10 years) whereas another may sell these alongside mortgages (20+ years). [½]
Other: The insurer may want to agree to a higher rate of commission with some advisers in order to retain their business, due to increased competition in the market. [½]
This may be more likely where the advisers are part of a large or national chain, leading to more negotiating strength. [½]
Higher commission may be paid where the insurer is in some form of a tie or other special relationship with the adviser. [½]
The insurer may put in place volume deals, whereby they agree to pay higher commission to brokers who bring in over £xm of business. [½]
Some brokers may do more administration, reducing the burden on insurers. [½]

(iii).question: Comment on the proposed new fee-based business model from the point of view of the customer.

Fee-based model - customer perspective: This structure enables the customer to see what they are paying for advice. [½]
It allows them to compare the services offered by different advisers, and the fees charged for those services. [½]
Customers should feel reassured that the choice of products is right for them and not influenced by commission rates. [½]
Customers may now find they need to find cash for an initial fee, which previously they would not have had to do. [½]
If customers lapse their policy they may lose some of the value invested in the initial fee. [½]
There is more administration for the customer, as they have outgo to both the insurer and the adviser. [½]
The customer may be shocked by how much advice costs, which may deter sales. [½]

(iv).question: Discuss the possible effects on the intermediaries of the move to a fee-based environment.

Initial commission: Advisers receiving a lot of initial commission are likely to see a significant fall in their income, at least initially. [½]
They will have to redesign their business model, focussing on customer service and retention, and with less emphasis on customer attraction. [½]
Renewal commission: Advisers currently receiving more renewal commission may not see a significant difference in income levels, provided the annual maintenance fee can be set at a similar level to the commission. [½]
All advisers: Fee-based business may be harder to sell to customers. [½]
This is because the amount the customer is paying for advice is transparent, which may put some customers off purchasing cover or make it unaffordable. [½]
The customer may even get the advice but then go and buy the policy directly rather than pay the adviser fee. [½]
Advisers will need to adapt to new sales methods and will need to reassess the products in the market to identify the most competitive products for customers. [½]

In order to attract customers, advisers may need to do more to win business, for example arranging appointments at evenings / weekends to suit customers, offering online advice tools for customers etc. [½]
In the long run, all advisers they may find their income stream is less volatile under the new regime, ... [½]
... as they can predict more easily what fees they will receive, ... [½]
... and will have more control over whether they do in fact receive those fees. [½]
There may be an overall fall in the advised-sales market, ... [½]
... as more customers seek more cost effective methods of obtaining cover. [½]
Advisers may lose business to directly written products, and 'cheap and cheerful' solutions such as bancassurers or white-labelled products sold in supermarkets. [½]
It may be more difficult for advisers to claim unpaid fees from customers than it was previously for insurers. [½]
Any disputes of this nature may be more time consuming as each one will need to be dealt with individually. [½]

(v).question:
As part of a regular audit process, a review of the claims management of the income protection portfolio is being carried out.

Describe how the claims management approach interacts with the other departments within an insurance company.

Pricing: Claim managers will provide an analysis of rates of claims incidence to feedback into any pricing exercise. [½]
They will also be required to provide an analysis of claim in payment to enable pricing actuaries to cost IP new business. [½]
Pricing actuaries will need to liaise with claims managers in order to understand the future strictness of the claim managers' approach so that this can be allowed for within the pricing process. [1]
For example, if claims managers are going to do more regular checks on claimants this would be expected to reduce the duration of claim and increase claim recoveries. [½]
However, it would also increase claims management costs that would also need to be considered. [½]
Underwriting: Claims managers will consider carefully the underwriting analysis performed at the time when the policy was accepted. [½]
Particular attention will be paid to the pre-existence of the condition for which the claim is now being made. In particular: [½]
to what extent was it linked to what was declared? [½]
The claims managers will need to liaise with underwriters, application-form designers and product developers to tighten wording to reduce anti-selection. [1]
Claims managers will investigate the loadings being applied by underwriters and compare these with the additional claims arising, to assess adequacy of these loadings. [½]
Product design: Claims managers would need to be fully aware of the product claim conditions, ... [½]
... both the legal context and any intended discretion above this. [½]
Claims managers would not want to incur unnecessary litigation costs through disputed claims. [½]
Equally, they would not want to be too lax in accepting claims and thus giving the wrong message to other (current and potential) policyholders. [½]
Experience analysis: Claims managers would be part of the process of in-depth analysis of experience to feed into: [½]
the reports on profitability for management [½]
reporting on state of company for regulatory solvency purposes [½]
... so that appropriate contract terms can be offered to policyholders for forthcoming new business. [½]
the preparation of profit assessments for tax purposes [½]
the process of reviewing existing policies if this is permissible. [½]

(vi).question:
As part of the investigation, you have been asked to review the following specific cases:

Case A: Claimant is a relatively new teacher who is suffering from a stress-related condition. Unable to work as a teacher and is claiming under an 'own occupation' definition. Disability counsellor advises that claim is genuine and claimant keen to return to some form of work.

Case B: Claimant has been in local press being awarded a prize for being leading scorer in local football league. Has been claiming under an ADL definition.

Case C: Claimant has been suffering from a knee injury for 5 months. Is claiming under a linked claims policy condition on a policy with a 12-month deferred period as policyholder was previously absent from work for 15 months with a broken hip. Policyholder is a manual labourer under an 'any occupation' definition.

Case D: Claimant is unable to undertake any work due to a degenerative heart condition, which was known to the claimant at the time the policy was put in force. The claimant is a member of a group income protection scheme that applies an 'any occupation' definition and has a free cover limit in excess of the claimant's salary.

Comment on the key aspects of each claim together with the options available to manage the claim in payment.

A Teacher, stress-related claim: This claim has the potential to be a very long-term claim, coupled with a high level of claim. [½]
Stress is not uncommon in the teaching profession. [½]
The claim appears to be genuine and so the insurer should continue to pay it. [½]
In order to facilitate a return to the claimant's occupation, the insurer could offer support through counselling to address the stress-related condition. [½]
Alternatively, if a return to the teaching profession does not appear to be possible, the insurer could offer a one-off cash payment to fund a career change. [½]
This could be a lump sum to fund the career change coupled with some form of compensation for any reduction in earnings expected compared to the previous occupation. [½]
B Claimant playing football, ADL based c: As the claimant has been in the press recently and won an award for scoring football goals, this claim appears to be fraudulent. [½]
The insurer would need to establish with certainty that the policyholder is the same person as the one mentioned in the press. [½]
If this can be confirmed, the insurer should cease payments with immediate effect. [½]
It may be that the claim started as a valid claim but the claimant has not informed the insurer as their situation has changed. [½]
The insurer could then take steps to recover income payments made after the insured recovered. [½]
C Linked claim for knee injury after bro: The insurer should validate that hip and knee claims are linked. [½]
However, this doesn't seem unreasonable and so the claim may be valid. [½]
The insurer should also consider how the knee injury occurred. [½]
For example, it may have occurred due to an accident at work and therefore potentially be unrelated to the previous claim for a broken hip. [½]
As the definition is 'any occupation' the insurer will need to establish that injured knee prevents any form of work. [½]
It may be the case that the claimant could carry out a more sedentary job such as in an office. [½]
If some form of work would be possible the insurer should cease to pay the claim with immediate effect. [½]
However, if the injury prevents all work, the claim should continue to be paid. [½]
The insurer should ensure that an appropriate programme of recuperative care is undertaken. [½]
D Claim due to pre-existing heart condit: The level of cover is under the free cover limit so there was no obligation for the medical condition to be disclosed. [½]
There may have been an actively at work clause in the group contract regarding the start of cover. [½]
If this is the case, the insurer will need to establish if claimant was at work at the time of the policy being effected. [½]
If the insured was not, the claim should cease immediately. [½]
If the insured was actively at work, or there was no clause, then the claim will need to continue to be paid. [½]
The insurer could check whether there is any individual IP insurance, or group IP insurance from a previous employer, that may also be paying out. [½]
The options available to the insurer will depend on the life expectancy of the individual. [½]
If the condition is not expected to be significantly life-limiting, the insurer could offer a lump sum payment to allow a change of lifestyle. [½]
